input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin maintains a real‑time, data‑driven representation of the physical asset. Live measurements are captured from the asset and streamed continuously to the twin using the MQTT protocol, enabling near‑real‑time synchronization between physical and digital components. The temporal granularity follows individual measurement events, and latency is kept minimal to support real‑time decision making.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system architecture follows the Digital Shadow Reference Model, which defines concepts such as data traces, processing components, and engineering or simulation models together with their associations. Concrete realizations are created by mapping these reference concepts to specific classes and components using MBSE languages (e.g., SysML) and architecture description languages (e.g., ACME, AADL). Active processing components consume input data traces, apply the associated models, and produce output traces, establishing a traceable data pipeline. This orchestrated structure integrates heterogeneous models and tools while supporting fine‑grained traceability across the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system employs Model‑Based Systems Engineering using SysML to capture structural aspects (components, interfaces, connectors) and uses MathWorks Simulink executable simulation models to represent behavioral and analytical dynamics. The digital shadow is defined as a set of contextual data traces collected from the physical object, aggregated and abstracted for a specific purpose. Data management relies on traceability links that bind these contextual traces to the corresponding parts of the SysML model and to the Simulink simulation, enabling consistent transformation and reuse. Thus the constellation comprises: (i) a SysML model (logical/functional architecture), (ii) Simulink models (physics‑based, executable), and (iii) the collected data traces that feed the models; relationships are one‑way from physical to digital shadow, while a full Digital Twin would add active components to close the loop.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through systematic traceability and verification of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models). Validation includes checking the correctness of both the white‑box Simulink simulation and the black‑box machine‑learning components against recorded robot state data. Uncertainty sources such as sensor noise and model approximation errors are acknowledged, with a need for quantification and error‑correction mechanisms. The reliability of the Digital Twin relies on rigorous verification of model transformations and continuous verification of data integrity, as emphasized for digital shadows.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The digital shadow is defined as an automated one‑way data flow from the physical object's state to a digital object, consisting of contextual data traces that are aggregated and abstracted for a specific purpose. The documents do not provide concrete data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data‑quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The paper states that a Digital Twin can provide active components that interpret and process incoming data and may push information back to the physical asset, unlike a digital shadow. No concrete command types, message formats, validation, authentication, feedback loops, conflict‑resolution procedures or execution confirmations are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process for creating the Digital Twin follows a Model‑Based approach: (1) selection of modeling languages (SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs) and methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT); (2) definition of a Digital Shadow Reference Model that captures contextual data traces, metadata, and processing components; (3) concretization of this reference model by mapping reference concepts to concrete classes and associations; (4) implementation of processing components that consume input data traces and produce output traces using either simulation or data‑calculation models; (5) traceability is maintained via the 'originates‑from' association, enabling transitive linkage of generated data to source components, input traces, and employed models. Versioning and evolution are managed through model refinements and re‑concretization, although specific version‑control tools are not mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Processing component described in the reference model performs computational steps that consume raw data traces, apply engineering, simulation or data‑calculation models, and emit derived output traces. These output traces constitute higher‑level abstractions that can be analyzed to generate insights about the physical asset (e.g., performance metrics, anomaly indicators). Because each derived trace retains provenance via the ‘originates‑from’ link, analysts can assess the confidence of recommendations by inspecting the underlying models and input data. The documented architecture therefore supports decision‑making through traceable, model‑driven analytics, although specific user‑interface mechanisms are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin design adheres to several industry‑accepted modeling standards: SysML (including state‑machine specifications in version 1.7) is used for structural and behavioral engineering models; architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc are employed for component‑connector representations; the reference‑model approach relies on a conformance relation that defines which concrete models conform to the abstract domain concepts. No additional certification or interoperability standards are explicitly referenced.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)
[12-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

[66] H. M. Muctadir, D. A. Manrique Negrin, R. Gunasekaran, L. Cleophas,
M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct
[2024. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z)

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

block_3_judge.reasoning:
The extraction is highly accurate and comprehensive, directly supported by the source text. It correctly identifies the use of SysML for structural architecture and Simulink for behavioral simulation, defines the digital shadow using the paper's specific terminology, and describes the traceability links and one-way data flow.

Supporting quotes:
- 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML [17]'
- 'Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink [28]'
- 'a digital shadow as "a set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose"'
- 'fine-grained traceability [16]'

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the physical acting components of the robot's first axis: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor... a gear transmission is first used... followed by a cycloidal gear'. Since relevant information is present in the document, this extraction is incorrect.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text provides details on the sensing infrastructure: 'There are two position encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.' It also mentions data collection via 'time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT'. Therefore, the claim that this is not in the document is incorrect.

The extraction accurately describes the validation approach based on comparing simulated data against measured data and the importance of traceability. It correctly reflects the paper's discussion on uncertainty (e.g., positional uncertainty) and the challenges of modeling accuracy. It is specific and well-grounded in the provided text.

Relevant quotes: 'The purpose... of the deviation of simulated data versus measured data', 'traceability allows for a meaningful linking of all data', 'sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional uncertainty', 'challenging to model accurately... promises a more accurate friction modeling'.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction incorrectly states that the documents do not provide concrete data types or communication protocols. Section IV.A(4) explicitly lists specific data types ('input current', 'output angle', 'torque', 'angular velocity', 'acceleration') and mentions that data is 'streamed via MQTT'. The extraction also claims a lack of preprocessing steps, but the text describes deriving values using a 'dynamics model'. Therefore, the assertion of absence contradicts the source text.

The description accurately reflects the text's distinction between a digital shadow and a digital twin, noting that a 'Digital Twin (DT) can provide such active components' while a shadow 'neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system'. It correctly assesses that specific technical details regarding control loops, command formats, and execution confirmations are not described in the document, which focuses on the passive shadow implementation.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text in Section IV.A(4) mentions that sensor data is 'streamed via MQTT'. As 'communication protocols' are a required part of the characteristic description, this constitutes relevant information present in the source. Thus, the claim of absence is incorrect.

The source text mentions 'visualization in a dashboard' and 'Asset Administration Shells' but does not describe hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational and storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, or deployment models. Therefore, the statement 'Not in Document' is accurate.

modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:
